prompt: For an unresponsive adult who is not breathing normally and has no pulse, rescuers should:

options.text: Begin high-quality CPR starting with chest compressions and use an AED as soon as available
Wait for advanced providers before any compressions
Give only rescue breaths for 5 minutes first
Check a pulse for at least 30 seconds every cycle without compressing

explanation: Adult cardiac arrest care prioritizes immediate compressions and early defibrillation.

distractorExplanations.c: Compression-first (C-A-B) is current adult approach for sudden arrest.
distractorExplanations.d: Pulse checks should be brief (≤10 seconds).

prompt: The recommended adult compression rate is:

options.text: 40 to 50 per minute
100 to 120 compressions per minute
160 to 180 per minute
As slow as possible to avoid fatigue

explanation: AHA targets 100–120/min with adequate depth and full recoil.

distractorExplanations.a: Too slow reduces cardiac output support.
distractorExplanations.d: Fatigue is managed by switching compressors ~every 2 minutes.

prompt: Adult compression depth should be at least:

options.text: 0.5 inch only
4 inches routinely
2 inches (5 cm), not exceeding about 2.4 inches (6 cm)
Depth does not matter if rate is correct

explanation: Adequate depth is a key high-quality CPR metric; excessive depth can cause injury.

prompt: Allowing complete chest recoil means:

options.text: Keeping continuous pressure on the sternum
Bouncing hands off the chest wildly
Compressing only the abdomen
Letting the chest return to normal position between compressions without leaning

explanation: Leaning reduces venous return; full recoil improves CPR quality.

distractorExplanations.b: Hands should stay in contact without bouncing off.
distractorExplanations.c: Hand position is lower sternum, not abdomen.

prompt: The compression-to-ventilation ratio for single-rescuer adult CPR (without advanced airway) is:

options.text: 100:0 always without exception

explanation: Adult CPR uses 30 compressions to 2 breaths for 1 or 2 rescuers before advanced airway.

distractorExplanations.b: 15:2 is used for 2-rescuer infant/child CPR in many AHA algorithms.
distractorExplanations.d: Compression-only is acceptable for untrained/ unwilling breathers, but trained BLS includes 30:2 when giving breaths.

prompt: When an AED arrives for an adult in cardiac arrest, you should:

options.text: Finish a full 10-minute CPR cycle before attaching pads
Power on the AED, apply pads, and follow prompts; resume CPR immediately after shock or no-shock advice
Wet the chest thoroughly before pad placement for better stick
Place both pads on the abdomen

explanation: Early defibrillation is critical for shockable rhythms; minimize pauses.

distractorExplanations.c: Dry the chest; water conducts and may spark.
distractorExplanations.d: Standard pad positions are anterolateral (or approved alternative).

prompt: If the AED advises “no shock,” the rescuer should:

options.text: Stop all care and wait
Shake the patient vigorously for a minute
Resume CPR immediately starting with compressions
Remove pads and leave

explanation: No-shock advice still requires high-quality CPR and reassessment per prompts.

distractorExplanations.b: Do not delay CPR with prolonged shaking.

prompt: Hand position for adult compressions is:

options.text: Fingertips on the clavicles
Hands over the upper abdomen only
One hand on each shoulder
Heel of one hand on the center of the chest (lower half of sternum), other hand on top

explanation: Correct hand position maximizes effective compressions and reduces injury risk.

distractorExplanations.b: Abdomen is not the compression site.

prompt: To minimize interruption of chest compressions, rescuers should:

options.text: Coordinate roles, limit pulse/rhythm checks, and switch compressors efficiently about every 2 minutes
Stop CPR for 1 minute after every 5 compressions
Discuss lunch plans during pauses
Never use an AED because it pauses CPR

explanation: High chest compression fraction improves outcomes; brief planned switches beat long chaotic pauses.

distractorExplanations.c: Unrelated talk during critical pauses is poor teamwork.
distractorExplanations.d: AED use is essential despite brief analysis pauses.

prompt: Rescue breaths for adults should:

options.text: Be as forceful and fast as possible
Produce visible chest rise over about 1 second each, avoiding excessive ventilation
Be withheld forever if a pocket mask is unavailable and no alternative exists for a trained willing rescuer—without considering compression-only
Always include 10 breaths without compressions first

explanation: Overventilation increases intrathoracic pressure and decreases venous return.

distractorExplanations.c: Compression-only is preferred if unwilling/unable to ventilate; trained rescuers give breaths with barrier when possible.
distractorExplanations.d: Do not delay compressions for prolonged breath-first sequences in sudden adult arrest.

prompt: For a witnessed adult collapse in a public place with a phone available, an alone rescuer should typically:

options.text: Leave for 20 minutes to find a friend before calling
Start by giving abdominal thrusts only
Ensure scene safety, check responsiveness/breathing, activate EMS/get AED, then start CPR
Assume the person is sleeping and walk away

explanation: Rapid EMS activation and AED retrieval are core adult Chain of Survival links.

distractorExplanations.b: Abdominal thrusts are for choking, not primary for sudden collapse.

prompt: If the victim has a medication patch on the chest where an AED pad will go, you should:

options.text: Place the pad directly over the patch
Never use an AED if any patch exists anywhere on the body
Burn the patch off with the AED
Remove the patch and wipe the area dry before applying the pad

explanation: Patches can block energy and burn skin; remove and wipe residue.

distractorExplanations.b: AEDs are still used—just clear the pad site.

prompt: High-quality CPR includes limiting pauses ideally to:

options.text: Less than 10 seconds when possible for critical interventions
30–60 seconds routinely for convenience
No compressions until ALS arrives if ALS is 20 minutes away
Pauses after every single compression

explanation: Short pauses preserve coronary perfusion pressure.

distractorExplanations.c: BLS must continue until ALS/relief/ROS C criteria.
distractorExplanations.d: Continuous high-quality compressions are the goal.

prompt: An adult with agonal gasps should be treated as:

options.text: Having effective normal breathing
Not breathing normally—start CPR if unresponsive and pulseless per assessment
Only needing a blanket
Ineligible for AED use ever

explanation: Agonal gasps are not adequate breathing and often occur in cardiac arrest.

distractorExplanations.c: Warmth alone does not treat arrest.

prompt: Team members should switch chest compressors approximately every:

options.text: 20 minutes
Every 2 compressions
2 minutes (or when prompted by AED/team leader) to reduce fatigue
Never switch once started

explanation: Fatigue quickly degrades depth and rate; planned switches maintain quality.

prompt: For child CPR (1 year to puberty), compression depth should be:

options.text: About one-third the depth of the chest, approximately 2 inches (5 cm)
Less than 1 cm always
At least 3 inches routinely for all children
Depth is irrelevant in children

explanation: Pediatric depth targets ~⅓ chest depth (~2 inches in children).

prompt: Two-rescuer CPR ratio for infants and children (without advanced airway) is commonly:

options.text: 30:2 only forever

explanation: AHA uses 15:2 for 2-rescuer infant/child CPR; 30:2 for single rescuer.

prompt: Infant chest compressions may be performed with:

options.text: A fist to the face
Heel of both adult hands stacked as for large adults without adaptation
Two fingers (1 rescuer) or two-thumb–encircling hands technique (2 rescuers)
Only abdominal thrusts

explanation: Infant techniques protect the small thorax while generating adequate depth (~1½ inches / 4 cm).

distractorExplanations.b: Adult two-hand technique is for larger victims.
distractorExplanations.d: Abdominal thrusts are not infant CPR compressions.

prompt: Infant compression depth target is approximately:

options.text: 0.25 inch
3 inches
5 inches
1.5 inches (4 cm)

explanation: Infants need about 1½ inches / ⅓ chest depth.

prompt: If you are alone and find an unresponsive infant after an unwitnessed collapse, after ~2 minutes of CPR you should:

options.text: Activate EMS/get AED if not already done, then continue CPR
Never call EMS for infants
Stop CPR permanently
Only give milk

explanation: Pediatric sequences emphasize brief CPR before leaving to call if alone and collapse unwitnessed; follow current AHA course details for phone/AED steps.

prompt: For children, cardiac arrest is more often preceded by:

options.text: Only myocardial infarction identical to older adults always
Respiratory problems / hypoxia than primary sudden cardiac causes (though both occur)
Hyperthermia treatment with ice baths as first CPR step
Elective skipping of airway support

explanation: Pediatric Chain of Survival emphasizes prevention and oxygenation; still provide compressions for pulseless arrest.

distractorExplanations.a: Primary cardiac events are less common but possible.
distractorExplanations.c: Ice baths are not the CPR algorithm start.

prompt: When using an AED on a child, if pediatric pads/attenuator are unavailable:

options.text: Never defibrillate a child under any circumstance
Cut adult pads in half
Use adult pads; do not let pads touch each other
Place both adult pads stacked on the umbilicus

explanation: Adult AEDs may be used if pediatric system unavailable; pad placement must not overlap.

distractorExplanations.b: Do not modify pads by cutting.
distractorExplanations.d: Follow anteroposterior or standard positions as trained.

prompt: Infant rescue breaths should:

options.text: Be maximally forceful
Be avoided for all infants in arrest even by trained rescuers with a barrier
Be given at 40 breaths without compressions first for 10 minutes
Be gentle enough to produce chest rise, covering mouth and nose with the rescuer’s mouth if using mouth-to-mouth-and-nose

explanation: Overventilation is harmful; visible chest rise is the guide.

distractorExplanations.b: Trained rescuers provide ventilations in pediatric BLS.
distractorExplanations.c: Do not delay compressions with prolonged breath-only periods in arrest.

prompt: Pulse check location commonly taught for infants is:

options.text: Brachial artery
Radial artery only as first choice
Popliteal only
Carotid only as the sole infant site taught

explanation: Brachial pulse is standard for infants; carotid/femoral used in older children.

distractorExplanations.c: Popliteal not the standard infant check.

prompt: For a child who is unresponsive and not breathing normally but has a pulse ≥60/min with poor perfusion signs, BLS may include:

options.text: No respiratory support ever
Rescue breathing with pulse checks, and start compressions if pulse remains <60 with poor perfusion per AHA pediatric guidance
Only abdominal thrusts continuously
Walking the child immediately

explanation: Pediatric bradycardia with poor perfusion is a compression indication even if a pulse is present but too slow.

prompt: Two-thumb encircling hands technique for infants is preferred when:

options.text: Never preferred
Only for adults
Two rescuers are available
Only during choking with effective cough

explanation: Two-thumb technique generally produces better depth/consistency with two rescuers.

distractorExplanations.a: It is preferred for 2-rescuer infant CPR.
distractorExplanations.d: Effective cough means observe, not CPR thumbs.

prompt: Compression rate for infants and children is:

options.text: 60 per minute
30 per minute
200 per minute
100 to 120 per minute

explanation: Same target rate as adults: 100–120/min.

prompt: If a child has a pulse and is breathing adequately after a brief seizure, you should:

options.text: Protect airway, place recovery position if appropriate, monitor, and activate EMS as indicated by condition/history
Start chest compressions immediately despite pulse and breathing
Give nothing and leave the child alone prone face-down in water
Force oral fluids while still postictal and unsafe airway

explanation: CPR is for arrest/poor perfusion states; postictal care is supportive with EMS per situation.

distractorExplanations.b: Do not compress if adequate pulse/breathing.

prompt: For infant CPR landmarking, compressions are delivered:

options.text: On the xiphoid tip intentionally
Just below the nipple line on the sternum
On the forehead
On the femur

explanation: Correct landmarking avoids abdominal organ injury.

distractorExplanations.c: Forehead is for airway positioning, not compressions.

prompt: Pediatric AED pad placement should:

options.text: Overlap pads completely for stronger shock
Place pads on clothing without skin contact
Follow manufacturer guidance; ensure pads do not touch (use AP placement if needed on small chests)
Be skipped if the child is wet without drying attempt

explanation: Pad contact and separation matter for effective, safe defibrillation.

distractorExplanations.d: Dry the chest; moisture is hazardous.

prompt: A responsive adult with severe choking (cannot speak/cough effectively) needs:

options.text: Abdominal thrusts (Heimlich) until object expels or victim becomes unresponsive
Encourage only weak coughing without intervention when severe
Blind finger sweeps routinely
Immediate AED application while they are still speaking in full sentences

explanation: Severe FBAO requires thrusts; mild obstruction with effective cough—encourage coughing.

distractorExplanations.d: AED is for cardiac arrest, not talking victims.

prompt: If a choking adult becomes unresponsive, you should:

options.text: Continue abdominal thrusts on an unresponsive victim upright
Lower to a firm surface, begin CPR, and look for an object in the mouth before breaths when visible
Leave to call without starting compressions if alone with a phone on speaker already activated—never start CPR
Pour water into the mouth

explanation: Unresponsive choking transitions to CPR; remove visible objects only—no blind sweeps.

distractorExplanations.a: Unresponsive victims get CPR on the ground.
distractorExplanations.c: CPR is indicated; use speakerphone if alone.

prompt: For a responsive choking infant, the correct sequence includes:

options.text: Abdominal thrusts as first-line identical to adults
Blind finger sweeps every cycle
5 back blows followed by 5 chest thrusts, repeating until relieved or unresponsive
Only watching for 10 minutes

explanation: Infant FBAO uses back blows and chest thrusts—not abdominal thrusts.

distractorExplanations.a: Abdominal thrusts are not for infants.

prompt: Mild airway obstruction with a strong cough should be managed by:

options.text: Immediate abdominal thrusts while they are effectively coughing
Starting CPR compressions while they stand and talk
Giving rescue breaths forcefully against resistance
Encouraging continued coughing and monitoring closely

explanation: Do not interfere with effective cough; be ready if it becomes severe.

prompt: Chest thrusts instead of abdominal thrusts are preferred for choking in:

options.text: Pregnant or markedly obese responsive adults (as trained)
All toddlers as the only method ever
Anyone with a mild cough only
Infants using adult Heimlich belts

explanation: Pregnancy/obesity alter landmarking; use chest thrusts per BLS training.

distractorExplanations.b: Infants use back blows/chest thrusts sequences.
distractorExplanations.d: Infants do not use adult Heimlich belts.

prompt: Suspected opioid overdose with respiratory arrest but a pulse requires:

options.text: Chest compressions only with no ventilation consideration
Rescue breathing/ventilatory support and naloxone per local protocols, plus EMS activation
Waiting for the person to wake without calling EMS
Giving syrup of ipecac

explanation: Opioid toxicity causes respiratory depression; support breathing and use naloxone when indicated/available.

distractorExplanations.a: Ventilation is critical if pulse present with apnea.

prompt: If an opioid overdose victim is unresponsive, not breathing normally, and pulseless:

options.text: Only give naloxone and never compress
Avoid AED because opioids contraindicate shocks
Start CPR and use AED; administer naloxone as soon as available without delaying compressions
Place in recovery position and leave

explanation: Cardiac arrest care still follows CPR/AED; naloxone is adjunctive, not a reason to skip BLS.

distractorExplanations.b: AED is still used for shockable rhythms.
distractorExplanations.d: Do not leave an arrest victim.

prompt: Naloxone (intranasal/autoinjector) in BLS settings is intended to:

options.text: Treat hypoglycemia as first-line
Replace the need for EMS
Defibrillate the heart
Temporarily reverse opioid-induced respiratory depression

explanation: Effects may wear off; monitor and be ready to redose per protocol; continue airway support.

prompt: After successful naloxone response, the person may:

options.text: Become agitated or re-sedate later as naloxone wears off—continue observation/EMS care
Be safely left alone immediately every time
Never need airway monitoring again
Be given more opioids to “balance” naloxone

explanation: Renarcotization is a known risk; observation is mandatory.

distractorExplanations.d: Do not give opioids to counter naloxone.

prompt: A choking child who becomes unresponsive should receive:

options.text: Continued standing Heimlich only
CPR with checks for visible objects before breaths
No compressions ever
Blind deep finger sweeps every time

explanation: Same transition to CPR as adults/children when unresponsive from FBAO.

prompt: Signs of severe foreign-body airway obstruction include:

options.text: Loud forceful coughing with ability to speak full sentences
Normal skin color and playful talking
Inability to speak, weak/silent cough, cyanosis, universal choking sign
Ability to sing loudly

explanation: Differentiate mild vs severe to choose observation vs thrusts.

distractorExplanations.d: Singing implies airway patent enough for mild category.

prompt: For pregnant choking victims, hand position for chest thrusts is:

options.text: Low on the fundus pushing toward the spine only as abdominal Heimlich
On the lumbar spine from behind without chest contact
On the forehead
Center of the sternum (same general area as CPR compressions)

explanation: Chest thrusts avoid gravid abdomen; follow BLS training details.

distractorExplanations.a: Standard abdominal thrusts are problematic in late pregnancy.

prompt: Scene safety with suspected opioid overdose includes:

options.text: Awareness of fentanyl exposure myths vs real risks; use PPE as trained and avoid needle sticks
Tasting unknown powders to identify them
Sharing the victim’s pills to confirm opioid type
Ignoring sharps on the ground

explanation: Rescuers protect themselves while providing rapid care; incidental skin contact is not the main panic narrative—avoid mucous membrane/sharps exposure.

prompt: High-flow oxygen / advanced airway management beyond BLS scope should be:

options.text: Performed by any bystander without training
Left to trained ALS providers; BLS focuses on basic ventilation and compressions
A reason to delay CPR for 15 minutes
Required before calling 911

explanation: Know your scope; high-quality BLS bridges to ALS.

prompt: If abdominal thrusts fail and the adult remains responsive but still severely choking, you should:

options.text: Stop trying after one thrust
Give the victim a large glass of water under pressure
Continue cycles of thrusts until the object is expelled or the victim becomes unresponsive
Start AED analysis while they are conscious and upright with a pulse

explanation: Persist with the choking algorithm; prepare to transition to CPR if unresponsiveness occurs.

distractorExplanations.d: AED is not for conscious choking with pulse.

prompt: Closed-loop communication means:

options.text: The receiver repeats back the order and the sender confirms correctness
Speaking only once without confirmation
Using vague pronouns for all tasks
Silence during resuscitation

explanation: Closed-loop reduces errors in high-stress codes.

prompt: A team leader’s role includes:

options.text: Performing every task alone without delegation
Assigning roles, monitoring CPR quality, and summarizing the plan
Discouraging AED use
Ignoring feedback from team members

explanation: Leadership improves coordination and chest compression fraction.

prompt: Constructive intervention means a team member should:

options.text: Remain silent even if compressions are on the abdomen
Wait until after ROSC documentation only
Speak up clearly if a critical error or unsafe action is about to occur
Argue personally without stating the clinical issue

explanation: Safety culture expects respectful challenge of dangerous actions.

distractorExplanations.b: Intervene in real time for critical errors.
distractorExplanations.d: Focus on the action, not personality.

prompt: During CPR with an advanced airway in place, ventilations are typically given:

options.text: 30:2 forever without change
No ventilations at all
1 breath every 60 seconds only
1 breath every 6 seconds (10/min) with continuous compressions without 30:2 pauses

explanation: Advanced airway allows asynchronous continuous compressions with timed breaths.

distractorExplanations.c: Every 6 seconds is the adult target.

prompt: Pregnant patient in cardiac arrest: in addition to high-quality CPR, rescuers should:

options.text: Perform manual left uterine displacement to relieve aortocaval compression when the uterus is clinically significant
Avoid AED use entirely in pregnancy
Place the patient fully flat supine without displacement consideration for a large gravid uterus
Delay compressions until obstetricians arrive if ETA is 30 minutes

explanation: LUD improves venous return; defibrillation is still indicated when needed; maternal resuscitation prioritizes CPR.

distractorExplanations.c: Gravid uterus can impair CPR without displacement.

prompt: If a rescuer is too fatigued to keep correct compression depth, the best action is:

options.text: Continue ineffective compressions silently
Announce the need to switch and rotate immediately with minimal pause
Stop the resuscitation permanently
Decrease rate to 40/min to rest

explanation: Teamwork corrects fatigue; pride should not degrade CPR quality.

prompt: Using a bag-mask device effectively usually requires:

options.text: Squeezing as hard and fast as possible
No head-tilt/chin-lift or jaw thrust consideration
Proper seal and often two-person technique for better ventilation
Placing the mask on the forehead only

explanation: Seal and airway position determine tidal delivery; avoid overventilation.

prompt: When to stop BLS efforts is generally determined by:

options.text: Personal inconvenience after 1 minute
Family requesting social media posts first
Guessing without assessment that “it’s fine”
Obvious signs of life/ROSC, transfer to ALS, valid DNR orders meeting local rules, or scene becoming unsafe

explanation: Know local protocols and legal/ethical frameworks for termination/withholding.

prompt: Special situation: victim on a soft bed—for CPR you should:

options.text: Move the victim to a firm surface or place a backboard if available
Compress deeply into a soft mattress without concern
Only do breaths on soft surfaces
Avoid CPR because beds forbid it

explanation: Soft surfaces dissipate force; firm support improves compression effectiveness.

distractorExplanations.d: CPR is performed with surface correction.

prompt: If the victim has a hairy chest and AED pads will not stick:

options.text: Place pads on top of thick hair without contact
Quickly remove hair in the pad area (razor if in kit) so pads adhere, then analyze
Skip AED permanently
Wet hair with oil

explanation: Pad contact is required for AED function; kits often include razors.

prompt: Water/scene: before AED use on a victim in a wet environment:

options.text: Apply AED while both rescuer and victim are in a puddle without drying
Never dry the chest
Move to a dry area if possible and dry the chest; avoid standing water around rescuers
Submerge the AED to ground it

explanation: Reduce shock hazard to rescuers; AEDs are used after reasonable drying/movement.

prompt: Role clarity during a code is improved when each member:

options.text: All crowd the airway with no compressor
Everyone gives contradictory orders simultaneously
Nobody watches the clock/AED prompts
Knows their assignment (compressions, airway, AED, documentation) and stays in lane unless reassigned

explanation: Clear roles prevent task fixation and missing compressions.

prompt: Feedback devices / CPR coaches help teams by:

options.text: Prompting rate, depth, recoil, and pauses in real time
Replacing the need for an AED
Diagnosing STEMI on their own as a 12-lead
Eliminating EMS activation

explanation: Quality metrics improve with coaching and feedback tools when available.

prompt: For a victim with a tracheostomy who needs ventilation, BLS-trained providers should:

options.text: Always seal the mouth only and ignore the stoma
Ventilate via the tracheostomy using appropriate interface as trained / per local protocol
Never ventilate tracheostomy patients
Pour liquid into the stoma

explanation: Ventilate the airway the patient has; follow training for stoma ventilation.

distractorExplanations.c: They need ventilation in arrest/respiratory failure.
distractorExplanations.d: Do not pour liquids into the airway.

prompt: After ROSC (return of spontaneous circulation), BLS providers should:

options.text: Stop all monitoring and leave
Immediately give the patient a large meal
Support airway/breathing, monitor pulse and blood pressure as able, and prepare for transport/ALS care
Remove AED pads and turn off attention to re-arrest risk

explanation: Re-arrest is common; continue close monitoring and supportive care.
